summary: The University of Washington was central to the efforts to ensure minority employment on public works projects in the late 1960s and early 1970s. During the construction of the big underground garage on campus, we faced a major protest that shut down the job to avoid violence. The university was the first to agree to a federal program under Executive Order 11246, requiring fair employment practices and weekly reporting of minority employment on campus projects. Despite challenges, the university remained committed to integrating minorities into the trades and served as a model for other contractors.

The Port of Seattle was another major public works employer where minority employment issues were raised, particularly by Tyree Scott. There were questions about the construction work at the port, and it was part of the broader conversation with contractors and unions about fair employment. Tyree and I, along with others like Dan Ruthford and Jim Wilson, worked to address these concerns and push for minority apprenticeships and fair hiring practices at the port and other sites.

The Central Contractors Association, or CCA, was the organization representing many contractors during this period. After the University of Washington garage protest, we began discussions with the CCA and the Associated General Contractors to negotiate minority hiring agreements. Dan Ruthford was the key negotiator from the contractors' side. The CCA was involved in multiple meetings and efforts to get contractors to comply with federal fair employment standards, though progress was slow and often met with union resistance.

The Associated General Contractors of America worked alongside the Central Contractors Association in these negotiations. They were part of the contractor organizations we engaged with to push for minority employment on public projects. Dan Ruthford represented them in meetings with Tyree Scott and others. While they made some efforts, the biggest challenges often came from union resistance rather than the contractors themselves.

Dan Ruthford was the main contractor negotiator during the minority hiring discussions. He sat in on numerous meetings with Tyree Scott, Jim Wilson, and others to try to find common ground with the unions and contractors. Ruthford was a key figure in working with the Chamber of Commerce and pushing the contractors to meet their obligations under federal programs. He was persistent and instrumental in keeping the dialogue going despite many setbacks.

Tyree Scott was a courageous and determined advocate for minority employment in construction trades. He led protests, including shutting down the University of Washington garage project and a Health Sciences construction site, to demand fair hiring practices. Despite facing threats, vandalism, and opposition even from within his own community, Tyree never gave up. He worked closely with contractors and government officials to open apprenticeship opportunities and was essential in pushing the unions to give ground.

Jim Wilson was involved alongside me, Dan Ruthford, and Tyree Scott in the negotiations and discussions about minority employment on construction projects. He was present at key meetings with contractors, unions, and government officials. Jim played a role in trying to mediate and find solutions during tense moments, such as the Health Sciences job shutdown. His participation helped maintain dialogue and push forward the efforts for fair employment.

Howard S. Wright Construction was the contractor on the Health Sciences project where Tyree Scott led a protest that shut down the job early one morning. We, including Jim Wilson, Dan Ruthford, and I, sat in a construction shack surrounded by police to negotiate an agreement that allowed work to resume. This incident highlighted the ongoing tensions but also the willingness of some contractors like Howard S. Wright to work toward fair employment solutions.

The Chamber of Commerce, led by Jim McCurdy at the time, was brought into the efforts to resolve conflicts between contractors, unions, and minority advocates. Jim McCurdy and I worked to get the Chamber involved to apply pressure and facilitate agreements. The Chamber played a role in meetings held in Olympia and Washington DC, helping to coordinate efforts among contractors and government officials to support minority employment programs.

Jim McCurdy was president of the Chamber of Commerce during this period and a key ally in pushing for minority employment on construction projects. He attended numerous meetings with contractors, unions, government officials, and advocates like Tyree Scott. Jim helped coordinate efforts to secure funding from the Department of Labor and was instrumental in applying political pressure to get the programs moving forward.

Olympia, Washington, was the site of several important meetings involving the governor, unions, contractors, and minority representatives. We met there multiple times to try to resolve the tensions around minority hiring in construction. One memorable meeting involved Austin St. Laurent walking out with several union leaders, showing the resistance we faced. These meetings were crucial in bringing all parties to the table and pushing for progress.

Austin St. Laurent was a union leader who was very resistant to the minority hiring efforts. I remember a meeting in Olympia where he got up and walked out, telling the governor he had another appointment, and seven other union leaders left with him. He fought vigorously against the changes we were trying to implement, and his opposition was a major obstacle. Despite this, we continued to push forward with the support of other leaders.

The Governor of Washington was involved in several meetings in Olympia aimed at resolving the conflicts over minority employment in construction. He tried to keep union leaders like Austin St. Laurent engaged during tense moments. The governor's office served as a venue where contractors, unions, and minority advocates came together to negotiate and seek solutions. His involvement helped elevate the issue to a statewide priority.

We met multiple times with officials from the United States Department of Labor in Washington DC to secure funding and support for minority training programs in Seattle. Arthur Fletcher and George Shultz were key figures we interacted with, though progress was slow and frustrating. We encountered resistance from lower-level department staff who seemed unsympathetic to our cause. Despite these challenges, we eventually secured a promise of $250,000 to $300,000 for training programs, though the funds were slow to materialize.

Arthur Fletcher was a Department of Labor official we met with in Washington DC during our efforts to promote minority employment in construction. He was one of the higher-level figures who supported our cause and helped articulate federal policies. However, despite his support, we faced obstacles from other department staff who controlled the actual funding decisions. Fletcher's involvement lent credibility to our efforts and helped keep the issue on the federal agenda.

George Shultz was another key federal official at the Department of Labor who we met with in Washington DC. Alongside Arthur Fletcher, Shultz helped enunciate the federal policies supporting minority employment under Executive Order 11246. However, the actual implementation and funding were often hindered by lower-level staff. Our meetings with Shultz were important in trying to push the Department of Labor to act decisively on our requests for training funds.

John Ehrlichman was a White House official we sought out when we were frustrated with the Department of Labor's slow response. Although Ehrlichman himself was not present when we arrived, his right-hand man, Todd Hallin, met with us and promised to try to influence the Department of Labor. This meeting gave us hope, though we were uncertain if it would lead to real change. It was also a unique experience to tour the White House and see the president's office during this visit.

The University of Washington Huskies came up during our White House visit because Todd Hallin, who met with us there, was a former quarterback for the Huskies. It was a small but memorable connection that made the meeting feel more personal. This link underscored the local ties involved in our efforts to advance minority employment in Seattle-area construction projects.

Todd Hallin was John Ehrlichman's right-hand man who met with us at the White House when Ehrlichman was called away. Hallin, a former University of Washington Huskies quarterback, spent about an hour discussing our minority employment concerns and promised to try to get Ehrlichman to act on the Department of Labor. Although we were hopeful, we weren't sure if this would lead to concrete results. The meeting was a significant moment in our ongoing struggle for fair employment.

Our visit to the White House was part of our effort to escalate the minority employment issue beyond the Department of Labor. Although Ehrlichman was not present, we met with Todd Hallin and were given a tour of the president's office and Cabinet Room. It was an impressive experience, but we remained uncertain whether the visit would translate into meaningful action. Still, it showed the seriousness of our campaign and the lengths we went to seek federal support.

Senator Warren Magnuson played a crucial role after our White House visit when we called his office to explain our difficulties. He invited us to meet in the Senate Appropriations Committee Room, where he and Senator Henry M. Jackson gathered several House members to hear our story. Magnuson was instrumental in pushing for a telegram to the Department of Labor demanding the release of training funds. His leadership and willingness to challenge the unions were vital to advancing our cause.

Senator Henry M. Jackson, known as Scoop, was present at the meeting convened by Warren Magnuson to address our minority employment concerns. Jackson was a strong supporter who helped rally House members to send a telegram to the Department of Labor demanding action. He was decisive and willing to confront union opposition, showing great respect for the cause. His involvement was a turning point in securing political pressure for funding and enforcement.

Tom Kelly was a House member involved in the discussions about minority employment funding, though he was not present at the key meeting with Warren Magnuson and Henry M. Jackson. He sent his first assistant to represent him. Kelly was part of the broader group of legislators who supported the effort to push the Department of Labor to release funds for training programs in Seattle.

Brock Adams was among the House members who attended the meeting with Senators Magnuson and Jackson to discuss minority employment issues. He was part of the bipartisan group that signed the telegram demanding the Department of Labor release funds for training programs. Adams's participation reflected the broad political support we garnered to address the systemic barriers minorities faced in construction trades.

Tom Foley was another House member present at the meeting with Senators Magnuson and Jackson, supporting the push for minority employment funding. His involvement helped create a united front among Washington state legislators to pressure the Department of Labor. Foley's support was part of the collective effort that led to the telegram demanding action on the training funds.

Executive Order 11246 was the federal mandate that required contractors on public works projects to provide fair employment opportunities to minorities. The University of Washington was the first to sign on to this program and enforce its provisions by reporting weekly on minority employment. We used the order as a legal basis to hold contractors accountable and push for training and apprenticeship programs. It was a critical tool in our fight to open the construction trades to minorities in Seattle.
